perturbation.original_text: HERC shall, throughout the term of this Agreement, obtain and maintain at its own cost and expense, from a qualified AAA-rated insurance company, a standard liability insurance and business interruption policy along with advertising injury 7 protection, all of which must be acceptable to THC and HSI, and which must name THC and HSI as additional insureds. Such policy shall provide, in addition to other protection, protection against any and all claims, demands, and causes of action arising out of any act, omission, negligence or otherwise giving rise to a third party claim. The amount of coverage shall be a minimum of three million dollars ($3,000,000) combined single limit, with no deductible amount for each single occurrence for bodily injury and/or property damage. HERC shall provide for ten (10) days notice to THC and HSI in the event of any modification, cancellation or termination. HERC agrees to furnish THC and HSI Certificates of Insurance evidencing same within thirty (30) days after the execution of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: HERC shall obtain and maintain liability insurance and business interruption policy, all of which must be acceptable to THC and HSI, and which must name THC and HSI as additional insureds. Such policy shall provide protection against claims arising out of an act, omission, negligence or otherwise giving rise to a third party claim. HERC agrees to furnish THC and HSI Certificates of Insurance evidencing same.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the requirement for a 'qualified AAA-rated insurance company', a 'standard liability insurance', and a specific minimum coverage amount of $3,000,000. The deletion of the minimum coverage requirements, and the omission of the strictures around the insurance companies involved creates a legal risk.
perturbation.law_explanation: New York Insurance Law § 2118 addresses insurance requirements and mandates certain minimum standards for liability insurance policies in the state. Removing specific coverage amounts and company rating requirements contradicts the intent of this law by allowing for potentially inadequate coverage, thus creating a legal vulnerability.

2025-03-28 2025-05-16 Share Facebook Email SECTION 2118 Excess line brokers; duties Insurance (ISC) CHAPTER 28, ARTICLE 21 § 2118. Excess line brokers; duties. (a) (1) Every licensee licensed pursuant to section two thousand one hundred five of this article shall be required to use due care in selecting the unauthorized insurer from whom policies are procured under his license. (2) (A) No policy of insurance may be procured by a licensee from any foreign or alien insurer which is controlled, by a foreign government or by a political subdivision thereof, or which is an agency of any such government or subdivision if the superintendent determines that: (i) such insurer receives a subsidy or other competitive advantage, as a result of such control or status, that would enable it to compete unfairly with similarly situated insurers which are not so controlled or constituted; (ii) such insurer is entitled to claim sovereign immunity as a result of such control and the insurer has not waived the sovereign immunity; or (iii) the use of such insurer would be detrimental to the interests of the people of this state. (B) No licensee shall be deemed to be in noncompliance with this subsection unless: (i) the superintendent has made a prior determination that the foreign or alien insurer from which the licensee procured a policy of insurance should not be used as an excess line insurer in this state in accordance with the provisions of this subsection; or (ii) the licensee knew or should have known that such insurer should not be used as an excess line insurer in accordance with the provisions of this subsection. The superintendent may promulgate regulations to provide guidance to the licensee. (C) Every such insurer shall otherwise satisfy all applicable requirements for placement by an excess line broker. * (b) (1) Within forty-five days after a policy is procured, a licensee shall submit the declarations page or cover note of every policy procured under his or her license to the excess line association established pursuant to se

perturbation.justification: The original text mandates a 'qualified AAA-rated insurance company' and a minimum coverage of $3,000,000, while the changed text omits these requirements. This creates a potential legal vulnerability. The law explanation suggests New York Insurance Law § 2118 addresses insurance requirements and mandates certain minimum standards for liability insurance policies, however the provided snippets of the law do not mention any minimum requirements for insurance policies. Even though the snippet provided does not explicitly mention minimum coverage requirements, the original text clearly defines those minimums which have been omitted in the changed text. Given the omission of the insurance company rating and coverage amount in the modified text, the change introduces the potential for inadequate coverage, creating a legal vulnerability. The removal of the specific coverage requirements significantly weakens the protections initially afforded in the original agreement. Therefore, a legal contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: During the Interim Period, neither HERC nor any of its affiliates or subsidiaries shall, directly or indirectly, engage in the business of renting or leasing cars, crossovers or light trucks (including sport utility vehicles and light commercial vehicles) in [any country in which THC or any of its affiliates or subsidiaries rents or leases cars, crossovers or light trucks (including sport utility vehicles and light commercial vehicles) as of the date of this Agreement] without THC's prior written consent, except to the extent materially consistent in type and scope with HERC's operations immediately prior to the date of this IPA.
perturbation.changed_text: During the Interim Period, neither HERC nor any of its affiliates or subsidiaries shall engage in the business of renting or leasing cars, crossovers or light trucks without THC's prior written consent.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the limiting clause, "except to the extent materially consistent in type and scope with HERC's operations immediately prior to the date of this IPA". This clause protects HERC from a blanket non-compete that could be construed as overly broad and anti-competitive. The removal of this clause is a legal risk.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified clause, without the exception for HERC's existing operations, could be interpreted as an unreasonable restraint of trade under Section 1 of the Sherman Antitrust Act, which prohibits contracts, combinations, and conspiracies that unduly restrict interstate or foreign commerce. The breadth of the non-compete, absent the limiting language, increases the risk of antitrust scrutiny.

Trusts, etc., in restraint of trade illegal; penalty Every contract, combination in the form of trust or otherwise, or conspiracy, in restraint of trade or commerce among the several States, or with foreign nations, is declared to be illegal. Every person who shall make any contract or engage in any combination or conspiracy hereby declared to be illegal shall be
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes a specific exception that allowed HERC to operate within the bounds of its existing business without requiring THC's consent. By removing this clause, the modified text broadens the scope of the non-compete agreement, which, according to the Law Explanation, could be interpreted as an unreasonable restraint of trade, potentially violating Section 1 of the Sherman Antitrust Act. Scraped Law Snippet 1 explicitly states that "Every contract, combination in the form of trust or otherwise, or conspiracy, in restraint of trade or commerce among the several States, or with foreign nations, is declared to be illegal." The original text provided a specific carve-out to avoid this issue, and its removal creates a potentially illegal restraint of trade.
